coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of polymeric fluorescent organic nanoparticles based on the self-polymerization of dopamine for biological imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Yingge
Jiang, Ruming
Liu, Meiying
Fu, Lihua
Zeng, Guangjian
Wan, Qing
Mao, Liucheng
Deng, Fengjie
Zhang, Xiaoyong
Wei, Yen
coredata.dc:description: Polymeric fluorescent organic nanoparticles (polymer-FONs) have raised considerable research attention for biomedical applications owing to their advantages as compared with fluorescent inorganic nanoparticles and small organic molecules. In this study, we presented an efficient, facile and environment-friendly strategy to produce polymer-FONs, which relied on the self-polymerization of dopamine and polyethyleneimine (PEI) in rather mild conditions. To obtain the final polymer-FONs, aldehyde group-containing copolymers (named as poly(UA-co-PEGMA)) were synthesized by reversible addition–fragmentation chain-transfer polymerization using polyethylene glycol methyl ether methacrylate (PEGMA) and 1-undecen-10-al (UA) as monomers. The dopamine was conjugated onto poly(UA-co-PEGMA) through a multicomponent reaction between UA and dopamine to obtain poly(UA-co-PEGMA)-DA, which was further utilized for preparation of polymer-FONs through self-polymerization of dopamine and PEI. 1H nuclear magnetic resonance, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy were employed to characterize the structure, morphology, compositions and optical properties of these polymer-FONs. Cell viability and cell uptake behavior results suggested that these polymer-FONs possess good biocompatibility and can be potentially utilized for biomedical applications. More importantly, the method can be also applied to fabricate many other multifunctional polymer-FONs with great potential for biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymeric fluorescent organic nanoparticles
Biological imaging
Dopamine
Self-polymerization
Biocompatibility

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Confocal microscopic imaging of cells using polymer-FONs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of polymer-FONs and the intermediate 1H NMR (400MHz).
TEM images of polymer-FONs with different magnificants, scale bar=100nm (A), and 50nm (B).
The FT-IR spectra of poly(UA-co-PEGMA) and polymer-FONs.
XPS spectra of polymer-FONs. (A) Survey scan ranging from 0 to 1200eV, (B) C1s region, (C) N1s region, (D) O1s region and (E) S2p region.

FL spectra of polymer-FONs (A) and the influence of pH to the fluorescence intensity (B). Optical image of aqueous solution of polymer-FONs were also shown in A, under natural light (left), and UV lamp (λ=365nm) (right).

Photostability of polymer-FONs in aqueous solution. The water suspensions of polymer FONs were continuously irradiated by UV lamp at 365nm for 1h. The fluorescent intensity values before and after irradiation were measured.
Cell viability of polymer-FONs with A549 cells (the concentrations of polymer-FONs are ranged from 20 to 100μgmL−1).
CLSM images of A549 cells when they were incubated with 20μg mL−1 of polymer-FONs for 3h. (A) excited with 405nm laser, (B) bright field, (C) merge image of A and B. Scale bar=20μm.
The preparation of poly(UA-co-PEGMA)-DA via MALI reaction.
The preparation of polymer-FONs in a gentle and efficient method.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of polymeric fluorescent organic nanoparticles based on the self-polymerization of dopamine for biological imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang 330031, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polymeric fluorescent organic nanoparticles (polymer-FONs) have raised considerable research attention for biomedical applications owing to their advantages as compared with fluorescent inorganic nanoparticles and small organic molecules. In this study, we presented an efficient, facile and environment-friendly strategy to produce polymer-FONs, which relied on the self-polymerization of dopamine and polyethyleneimine (PEI) in rather mild conditions. To obtain the final polymer-FONs, aldehyde group-containing copolymers (named as poly(UA-co-PEGMA)) were synthesized by reversible addition–fragmentation chain-transfer polymerization using polyethylene glycol methyl ether methacrylate (PEGMA) and 1-undecen-10-al (UA) as monomers. The dopamine was conjugated onto poly(UA-co-PEGMA) through a multicomponent reaction between UA and dopamine to obtain poly(UA-co-PEGMA)-DA, which was further utilized for preparation of polymer-FONs through self-polymerization of dopamine and PEI. 1H nuclear magnetic resonance, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy were employed to characterize the structure, morphology, compositions and optical properties of these polymer-FONs. Cell viability and cell uptake behavior results suggested that these polymer-FONs possess good biocompatibility and can be potentially utilized for biomedical applications. More importantly, the method can be also applied to fabricate many other multifunctional polymer-FONs with great potential for biomedical applications.

Polymeric fluorescent nanoparticles were fabricated via a novel strategy that combines the RAFT polymerization, multicomponent reaction and self-polymerization of dopamine.
Image 2

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, fluorescent nanomaterials have attracted considerable research attention as their outstanding properties and performances, such as quantum effect, high specific surface areas, small size, fluorescence, multifunctional potential and so on [1–4]. Therefore, they have been widely applied in communication [5–7], storage [8], biomedical imaging [10,23], diagnosis [11–13] and theranostics [14–16]. Among all these applications, biological imaging has played an important role and greatly promoted the development of fluorescent nanomaterials. Biological imaging can be used to monitor the biological processes and obtain the information of tumor growth and metastasis, development of infectious diseases and expression of certain genes, and improve the veracity and decrease the operation simultaneously [17]. Fluorescent nanomaterials can be divided into three parts: inorganic, organic and traditional fluorescent proteins. Traditional fluorescent proteins, for example green fluorescent protein series, red fluorescent proteins and independent light-emitting proteins, are poor in stability and membrane penetration [18]. The synthesis of these fluorescent proteins is also tedious and expensive. Fluorescent inorganic nanomaterials, including semiconductor quantum dots [19,20], carbon quantum dots [21–24], silicon quantum dots [25], upconversion nanoparticles [26] and metal nanoclusters [27], Ln ions doped luminescent nanomaterials [28,29] were perplexed by their toxicity and poor degradability. Therefore, some efforts in searching new methods should be done to deal with such dilemma.

Besides the highlights of biocompatibility, fluorescent organic nanomaterials (FONs) have advantages over fluorescent inorganic nanomaterials in the designability of structure and functions [30–32]. Zhou et al. created six new dyes adopted 2, 2-bipyridyl as electron acceptor and six other conjugated compounds as electron donors, which were promising in cell imaging as low toxicity [33]. Jang et al. prepared conjugated polymer dots using poly(diphenylacetylene) derivatives and commercial conjugated polymers following specific molecular design rules [34]. However, planar conjugated organic dyes involved in the aggregation-caused quenching effect (ACQ). Tang et al. discovered aggregation-induced emission materials can still keep high fluorescence in high concentration or solid state and are promising for bio-probes [35–38]. Meanwhile, it was largely limited by complex and multi-step experimental procedure, high cost and hydrophobic nature [39]. Therefore, it is desirable to develop green, efficient and economical methods in the synthesis of FONs. Dopamine (DA), an important molecule in mussel inspired chemistry, has been widely applied in catalysis [40], adsorption [41–43], antisepsis, surface modification, functional materials for controlled drug delivery and photothermal treatment [38,44,45,47–49], energy storage and conversion [50–52] as it occurred self-polymerization under alkaline condition [53–56]. In recent years, some fluorescent polydopamine (PDA) compounds, including PDA-based FONs [57], fluorescent PDA capsules [58] and fluorescent PDA-polyethyleneimine (PEI) copolymers [59,60], were reported successively. However, it is still challengeable to control their chemical compositions and functional groups of these PDA based FONs.

In this contribution, a novel strategy has been developed to fabricate PDA based FONs through the combination of reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer polymerization (RAFT) polymerization and self-polymerization of dopamine and PEI in rather mild conditions. First, the copolymers poly(PEGMA-co-UA) were prepared from RAFT polymerization using polyethylene glycol methyl ether methacrylate (PEGMA) and 1-undecen-10-al (UA) as monomers. Then dopamine was conjugated on poly(UA-co-PEGMA) through mercaptoacetic acid locking imine (MALI) reaction. The final polymer-FONs were formed by the self-polymerization of PEI and poly(UA-co-PEGMA)-DA. For verifying their potential biomedical applications, the biocompatibility and cell uptake behavior of polymer-FONs are also studied.

All of the chemical agents and solvents were used as received. Mercaptoacetic acid (MC), UA, 2, 2-Azobis (2-methylpropionitrile) (AIBN) and PEGMA were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Dopamine hydrochloride (DA) was purchased from Sangon Biotech. Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chain transfer agent was prepared as our previous report [61]. Other chemical agents were commercially available and of analytical grade. Deionized water (D.I. water) was used in the experiment. UV–Vis spectra were recorded on an ultraviolet and visible spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., China). Fluorescence spectra were measured on a Hitachi FS F-4500 spectrometer with a slit width of 5nm for both excitation and emission. The 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrum was recorded on a Bruker Avance-400 spectrometer with D2O and CDCl3 as the solvent. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a Hitachi 7650B microscope operated at 80kV; the TEM specimens were obtained by placing a drop of ethanol nanoparticle suspension on a carbon-coated copper grid. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded in a transmission mode on a Nicolet 5700 spectrometer (Waltham, MA, USA). The size distribution of polymer-FONs in water was determined by a Nano Zeta Potential and Submicron Particle Size Analyzer (Beckman Coulter Delsa).

The synthesis of poly(UA-co-PEGMA) was shown in Scheme 1 in detail. RAFT polymerization was carried out using AIBN as the initiator, UA and PEGMA as the monomers. Briefly, 504.84mg UA, 712mg PEGMA, 12.173mg AIBN and 9.7mg CTA were dissolved in 5mL ethyl acetate and then added into schlenk flask. The reaction systems were tightly sealed and then purged with dry N2 for 30min to remove the oxygen. Then, the schlenk flask was immersed in an oil bath at 65°C. After 20h, schlenk flask was moved into ice bath. Finally, the mixture was dialyzed for 24h to remove the solvent and monomer before drying in vacuum oven.

Poly(UA-co-PEGMA)-DA was synthesized via the MALI reaction (Scheme 2 ). Besides mild reaction conditions, MALI reaction possesses the superiority of high efficient and environmental friendliness with water as the only byproduct. In brief, 500mg poly(UA-co-PEGMA), 260mg MC, 250mg DA, and 5mL dimethyl formamide were put into 10mL round-bottom flask, then keep the mixture for 2h at room temperature. Finally, the mixture was dialyzed for 24h and dried in vacuum oven.

The procedure for preparation of polymer-FONs was displayed in Scheme 3 . In brief, 500mg poly(UA-co-PEGMA)-DA copolymers were dissolved into 3mL deionized water at room temperature before adding PEI (150mg). The solution was kept 2h under ambient temperature. Then, it can be got the target products, polymer-FONs were purified through dialysis in porous cellulose bag and drying in vacuum oven at 40°C for 24h.

The cell viability of polymer-FONs on human lung adenocarcinoma epithelial (A549) cells was determined by cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay based on our previous reports [62]. The detailed experimental procedure can be found in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal microscopic imaging of cells using polymer-FONs
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed information was provided in the supporting information.

The 1H NMR spectra of polymer-FONs and intermediate products were characterized to supply structural information. The monomers involved in the RAFT polymerization were UA and PEGMA which possessed characteristic peaks of proton. As shown in Fig. 1A, an obvious signal peak at 9.72ppm (a) was observed. It can be contributed to CHO of UA. Meanwhile, the triplet peak located at 3.66ppm (c) just corresponded to the signal peaks of methylene in PEGMA. The peak of b was from CDCl3. So, it can conclude the RAFT polymerization of UA and PEGMA had succeeded. Compared with Fig. 1A, another peak at 6.7–6.5ppm was found in Fig. 1B. It should be derived from the aromatic ring of DA. However, the peak at 9.72ppm was disappeared in Fig. 1B as the influence of DA and MC. Thus, DA has conjugated with UA through MALI reaction. D2O was chosen as solvent in the characterization of polymer-FONs. PEI is an important multi-amino compound, which is widely used in biomedicine. As shown in Fig. 1C, the characteristic peak of b-PEI (2.2–1.9ppm) was clearly detected, indicating the existence of PEI in polymer-FONs. Therefore, it suggested that polymer-FONs have been prepared as designed.

The TEM images provided the size and morphology information about these polymer-FONs. As shown in Fig. 2 , polymer-FONs were rather uniform and monodispersed. Overall, the surface was a bit rough but round. Under high magnification, it can accurately identify that these polymer-FONs were with an average diameter of about 50nm. As shown in Fig. S1, the dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiment has been carried out to determine the size distribution of PDA based FONs in PBS. The results showed the size distribution was about 57.4±23.7nm with a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.206. As the forming mechanism, it can be supposed to the chain entanglement as the forces between the hydrophilic segments and hydrophobic blocks. The optical properties of polymer-FONs were evaluated by UV–Vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. First, UV–visible spectroscopy was used to offer specific data to confirm the functional groups and composition of polymer-FONs. As shown in Fig. S2, the peak at 277nm was obviously observed. In view of the rough composition, the peak is probably derived from the transition of π→π* and n→π* in CO and CN. The above results indicated that Schiff base may exist in polymer-FONs and it could contribute to the fluorescence of the resultant FONs.

The XPS survey scan ranging from 0 to 1200eV was carried out to determine the elements in polymer-FONs. As shown in Fig. 4A, three characteristic peaks at 284.6, 397.8 and 531eV can be observed, which can be attributed to C, N and O, respectively. The high nitrogen content in the sample of polymer-FONs suggested that PEI was successfully connected with DA. In addition, the individual elements of polymer-FONs were further displayed in Fig. 4B–E, the peak at 161.7eV can be attributed to emergence of S, which could be observed in polymer-FONs, indicating DA was successfully conjugated with poly(UA-co-PEGMA) via MALI reaction. Based on XPS analysis, we can draw the conclusion that PDA based polymer-FONs were successfully synthesized.

Fluorescence spectra of polymer-FONs were shown in Fig. 5 . It can be seen that the emission wavelengths were gradually increased when the excitation wavelengths were increased from 340 to 410nm. Based on the excitation spectra, the optimal excitation wavelength was located at 375nm. The maximum emission wavelength of polymer-FONs was 505nm under the optimal excitation wavelength (375nm). The fluorescent results are well consistent with the optical images in the insets of Fig. 5. Uniform and green fluorescence can be observed when water suspensions of polymer-FONs were irradiated with UV lamp at 365nm (right bottle in insets). Fig. 5B shows the effect of pH values on the fluorescent properties of polymer-FONs. It can be seen that the emission peak of polymer-FONs has not obviously changed in different pH solution.

The photostability and quantum yield (QY) were also important to evaluate their performance in cell imaging. Herein, we further investigated the photostability and QY of polymer-FONs before the utilization in cell imaging. As displayed in Fig. 6 , the fluorescence intensity of polymer-FONs just had slight decrease after continuous irradiation for 1h under the ultraviolet lamp at 365nm. Besides, QY was also conducted and the fluorescence QY of polymer based FONs were reached up to 15.1%. Considering their excellent biocompatibility and fluorescence properties, thus polymer-FONs are promising for cell imaging application.

CCK-8 assay was carried to quantitatively evaluate the cytotoxicity of polymer-FONs. It was obvious that the viability of cells did not decline after incubated with different concentrations of polymer-FONs for 24h (Fig. 7 ). Even the concentration of polymer-FONs was as high as 100μgmL−1, the cell survival rate was still above 98.75±0.008%. Therefore, polymer-FONs owned favorable biocompatibility. Meanwhile, it can be demonstrated that the cell experiment had a good reliability. As the outstanding properties, for example, high fluorescent intensity, good water-solubility and simple preparation, polymer-FONs have broad application prospects in biomedicine fields.

To evaluate the potential biomedical applications of polymer-FONs, their cell uptake behavior was investigated by CLSM. A549 cells were seeded in culture with 20μgmL−1 of polymer-FONs. After 3h, the cell uptake of polymer-FONs was clearly observed in Fig. 8 . Fluorescence signal was visible in cytoplasm when cells were irradiated with 405nm laser. Meanwhile, the center locations of cells are absent of fluorescence signals. It suggested the polymer-FONs were uptake by cells through non-specific endocytosis and mainly accumulated in cytoplasm. The above speculation was further confirmed by the emerged image in Fig. 8C, which clearly showed that these polymer-FONs can be internalized by cells but cannot enter cell nuclei. Considered the facile and designability preparation procedure, high water dispersibility and good biocompatibility, these polymer-FONs should be of great potential for various biomedical applications.

In summary, polymer-FONs were designed and prepared combining RAFT polymerization and self-polymerization of DA and PEI. The prepared polymer-FONs were characterized by 1H NMR, TEM, FT-IR, and fluorescence spectroscopy. All of these characterization results demonstrated that these polymer-FONs possess high water dispersibility, strong fluorescence and desirable biocompatibility, making them very promising candidates for biological imaging applications. More importantly, the chemical compositions and functional groups of these polymer-FONs could be well adjusted through RAFT polymerization owing to its well monomer adaptability. It is therefore the method described in this work may potentially be utilized for fabrication of many other multifunctional polymer-FONs while combination of RAFT polymerization and self-polymerization of dopamine.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51363016, 21474057, 21564006, 21561022, 21644014), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province in China (Nos. 20161BAB203072, 20161BAB213066) and the National 973 Project (Nos. 2011CB935700).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2017.04.033.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: J. Polym. Sci. A Polym. Chem.
